archive_overview: This JSON catalogs concert, revue, and theatrical performance programs found in a digitized collection of personal papers belonging to (or collected by) Majer 'Ivan' Pietruschka, a Polish-born violinist and orchestra leader. Pietruschka played in the Warsaw and Lodz Symphony Orchestras and conducted a silent-film orchestra in Berlin (1923-1932) before Nazi restrictions on Jewish musicians drove him to England in 1939. In 1940 the British Government transported him and other refugees to Australia aboard the HMT/HMAT Dunera (attacked three times by German U-boats en route), where he was interned as an 'enemy alien,' first apparently at Hay Camp (NSW) and later at Tatura (Victoria). After release, he joined the Australian Army's 8th Employment Company, entertaining troops with an orchestra he formed, and after the war played with the 3DB Orchestra and, from 1952, the Melbourne Symphony Orchestra. He married pianist-composer Phyllis Batchelor in 1946 and settled in Heidelberg, Victoria. Many entries below document a remarkable, internally consistent internee arts community (documented across multiple internment sites and a repatriation voyage) that reused songs, personnel, and even the same fairy-tale conceit (Snow White) across several years and productions. Non-concert material in the source collection (personal letters, certificates, family photographs, unrelated artwork, sheet music with no accompanying event data, and art exhibition catalogs) was excluded from this catalog.

concerts.title: Snowhite Joins Up
alternate_titles: Snowhite: A Merry Xmas-New Year Revue
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia
concerts.date: New Year season 1941/1942 (Christmas-New Year revue, 1941 into 1942)

overall_credits.music: Ray Martin (partly based on popular tunes)

songs.title: The Fairy Wood
songs.credits: Cast: The Queen - O. Hans Mayer; The Master of the Bedchamber - Hugo Schuster; Prince Charming - Eric Liffmann; The Witch/Grandma - George Blank; Longnose - H. R. Risotto
songs.additional_notes: Opening fairy-tale scene establishing the Snow White frame story.

songs.title: The Banquet
songs.credits: Two Ambassadors - Baron de Reissn[er], Ernest Hutterer; Mr. Internee - Josef Almas; Two Ladies - George Blank, Ernest Hutterer; Grandson - Alfred Jablonsky; Santa Claus - uncredited/illegible. The Ballet: Soloist Klaus Begach; Alfred Jablonsky, Mops Major, Klaus Presser, Fred Rosenthal, Erich Schlesinger, Armin Stern. The 'Ex-Ray' Melody Makers: Peter Alsberg, Kurt Flussmann, Heinz Hermannsohn, Werner Hirschfeld, Heinz Lippmann
songs.additional_notes: Comic banquet scene introducing 'Mr. Internee' as a character - direct acknowledgment of the internment-camp setting within the fairy-tale frame.

songs.title: Broad Hay Melodies
songs.additional_notes: Segment title puns on 'Hay' (the camp) - likely a medley of the songs below.

songs.title: Hay Days
songs.composer_lyricist: Words & music not separately attributed on this document (see related manuscript, additional notes)
songs.lyrics:
Hay Days, Hay Days,
Make your Hay Days your play days
Sing on every day, doodle-oodle-aye,
Smile with never a care.
Hay Days, Hays Days,
Make your Hay Days your gay days,
Yours is all the fun,
Yours is all the sun,
For it shines everywhere.
Long as we're all pals
And we're together,
Never mind the rain and stormy weather,
I know, you know,
Troubles come but they do go,
Doors will open up, you'll be right on top.
Live your life your own way,
Calling every day: HAY DAY.
songs.additional_notes: A full handwritten manuscript draft of this song (with an additional verse continuation) survives separately in the collection, suggesting it was drafted specifically for this revue. The title puns on 'Hay' Internment Camp.

songs.title: Say Hay for Happy
songs.lyrics:
When you're feeling low-down,
And when you've had a blow-down,
Take it easy as it comes and goes.
When you're feeling chilly,
And everything looks silly,
Show that you can be right on your toes:
Say Hay for happy
When you feel snappy,
And you don't want to cry,
Say Hay for happy
When you feel snappy
And you don't want to die.
So you'd even sell your overcoat
For just a sight of one more bite
Of tasty "Butterbrot".
Blow all your troubles
Away like bubbles
And start a little game.
Without your girlie
You get up early,
Oh what an awful shame!
You're saying R for Rosenthal,
B for Blumenduft, and S for snappy -
Now say: HAY FOR HAPPY.
songs.additional_notes: Directly adapted/reused in the later 1943 revue 'Sergeant Snow White' as 'Eight's for 'Appy' (substituting 'Eight's', for the 8th Employment Company, for 'Hay'). The lyric names real fellow-internees Rosenthal and Blumenduft in a comic spelling-out gag.

songs.title: Don't Release Me, Mr. Layton
songs.lyrics:
[Refrain of the first 3 verses:]
Don't release me, Mr. Layton,
For in here it's paradise,
And if the words I said ain't true,
Then I'm just telling lies.

[Refrain of the last verse:]
Please release me, Mr. Layton,
That's my only wish to you,
And all the words I said just now are absolutely true!
songs.additional_notes: An ironic protest song addressed to a camp/immigration official named Layton, using sarcastic reverse psychology (praising camp life) to express a genuine longing for release. A fuller handwritten draft of this song survives separately in the collection with all 5 verses (see the standalone song entry 'Don't Release Me, Mr. Layton' below for complete verses).

songs.title: Good-bye to You, Australia
songs.lyrics:
Good-bye to you, Australia, land of sun and peace,
We're sailing to freedom over the seven seas.
When grey clouds are showing on top, let's get our kit-bags lined up:
We're sailing to freedom, with a "V FOR VICTORY" smile...
songs.additional_notes: Direct precursor of 'We Are Calling All Cobbers' / 'We Greet You Australia' in the 1943 revue 'Sergeant Snow White,' which reuses nearly identical lines with reversed sentiment (greeting Australia rather than bidding farewell), reflecting the men's changed circumstance from prospective deportees to enlisted soldiers.

songs.title: Snowhite's Almanach
songs.credits: Words by Fritz Gottfurcht, Rudolf Popper, Josef Almas, Egon Lehrburger, Ray Martin. Music by Alan Grey, Felix Popper, Frederic Hollaender, Eric Liffmann. Characters impersonated by Josef Almas, Leo Bieber, Hugo Schuster, Kurt Baier, Eric Liffmann, Peter Alsberg, Edward Nelken, H. R. Risotto, David Rummelsburg, Fritz Weidenbaum, Rudolf Laqueur.
songs.additional_notes: Titles of the 4 individual chansons within this segment were not given on the surviving page.

concerts.additional_notes: This is the direct predecessor of the 1943 Melbourne revue 'Sergeant Snow White' (see that entry), sharing the same writer-director (Doc K. Sternberg), composer (Ray Martin), the Snow White fairy-tale frame, and at least two reworked songs. Confirms the same community of internees carried their amateur theatrical tradition from Hay Camp (1941/42) through Tatura (1941-42, see related Tatura entries) to the 8th Australian Employment Company in Melbourne (1943). The document is incomplete: it breaks off partway through Part II and a likely Part III was not captured/preserved.

concerts.title: Don't Release Me, Mr. Layton (song manuscript)
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia
concerts.composer_lyricist: Unattributed on the manuscript (performed within 'Snowhite Joins Up', see that entry)
concerts.lyrics:
1) We sang, "You'll get used to it", and other songs of ease,
but there's one song we never sang: a song about release,
for things are simply marvellous and if they stay that grand,
We'll soon be at the cemetery beneath a layer of sand.
CHORUS: Don't Release me, Mr. Layton, for in here it's paradise
and if the words I said, ain't true, then I'm just telling lies. -

2) What do we care about the world or girlies if you please,
just outside there's a war on and in here we live in peace.
We're all just pals who never fight and if you're ever hit,
the guy just wants to make you see the southern stars a bit. (Chorus)

3) I wait and wait for letters, but I wait and wait in vain,
I write down all my troubles, but no word comes back again.
But today I had a letter saying "you can't feel so sad,
I hear each day that Hay conditions aren't so overbad." (Chorus)

4) With one word it's just marvellous so grand that I could cry
I wouldn't find a better place on earth for me to die.
I love each little day in here each fly within the air,
and if they tease me in the heat, oh gosh, what do I care!!! - (Chorus)

5) I think you all found out by now the lesson of this tune,
I know how much you're longing to be out of here quite soon.
I know there's just one single thing that moves us every day,
and so to Mr. Layton there's but one thing we can say:
EXTRA CHORUS (final): Please, release me, Mr. Layton, that's my only wish to you
and all the words I said just now, are absolutely true.
concerts.additional_notes: A satirical protest song by internees at Hay Camp, using sarcasm (elaborately praising camp conditions) to express a genuine desire for release, addressed to a 'Mr. Layton' (apparently a camp or immigration official). Performed (in shorter form) within the revue 'Snowhite Joins Up' (1941/42) - see that entry. Parodies the musical/rhetorical convention of a pleading 'release me' song.

concerts.title: Die Geschichte vom braven Soldaten Schwejk (The Story of the Good Soldier Schwejk)

overall_credits.based_on: The novel by Jaroslav Hašek, adapted for the stage by Rolf Stein

songs.credits: Leo Bieber

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Der traurige Mann (the sad man) - A. Rosenthal; Der wütende Mann (the furious man) - P. Gelbein; Der elegante Landstreicher (the elegant tramp) - E. Hutterer

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Polizeikommissar - E. Rosenthal; psychiatric authorities Prof. Blachhirn/Prof. Soolschreck/Prof. Schloch - W. Rechnitz, A. Ehrenzweig, H. Hirschberg/G. Blank

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Frau Mueller - F. Weidenbaum

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Patients Palivec/Pokorny/Kovarik - L. Abraham, G. Auer, J. Deutsch; Dr. Pimpfinger (Oberarzt) - H. Schuster; Ordonanz - F. Gelbein; Sanitäter - E. Rosenthal; Erzherzogin (Archduchess) - E. Hutterer

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Oberleutnant Lukasch - F. Schoonbach; Oberst Kraus - H. Schuster

acts.act_name: 7. Bild: Der Liebesbrief oder Kleine Ursachen - Grosse Wirkungen

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Palivec - L. Abraham; Kakonyi, Eisenhändler - J. Deutsch; Mizzi, Dienstmädchen - W. Frey; 1. Eskorte - E. Freind; 2. Eskorte - P. Gelbein; Zeitungsjunge - A. Rosenthal

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Oberleutnant Lukasch - F. Schoonbach; Exzellenz von Schwarzburg - B. Kardegg; Kontrolleur - G. Auer; Würstelverkäufer - A. Rosenthal

songs.title: Scene
songs.credits: Der Bauer - E. Rosenthal; Stimme des Oberleutnant Lukasch - F. Schoonbach

songs.title: Epilog
songs.credits: Leo Bieber

concerts.additional_notes: A full-length dramatic adaptation of Hašek's satirical novel (distinct from, and much more extensive than, the brief 'Schweyk' comic sketch later included in 'Sergeant Snow White,' 1943). Confirms M. Pietruschka (orchestra leader), Hans W. Katz (composer), and Emil Wittenberg (set designer) were part of the Tatura internee arts community about 18 months before the same individuals' involvement in the Melbourne productions of the 8th Australian Employment Company.

concerts.title: Wir Reisen um die Welt / We Travel Round the World
concerts.date: c. 1941-42 (undated; contemporaneous with other Tatura productions)

acts.act_name: Full cast (German and English programme versions)

concerts.additional_notes: This revue, structured as a comic 'world tour' (through France, Hungary, Venice, Berlin, Vienna, Hawaii, and a Transport Ministry sketch), survives in the collection as both a German-titled programme ('Wir Reisen um die Welt') and an English-translated programme ('We Travel Round the World,' subtitled 'A Revue without a girl' - an acknowledgment of the all-male internment-camp cast), with identical cast and crew. Two of its songs ('Ich hob' a Gspusi' and 'Pour t'embrasser je donnerai ma vie') were performed as excerpts in the dated concert 'Grosses Unterhaltungskonzert' (Tatura, 20 January 1942, see that entry), which places this revue at or before that date and confirms its Tatura origin. Multiple decorative cover variants survive, one reading 'Eine Revue in 10 Stationen' versus the interior programme's 'Eine Revue in 9 Bildern' (9 scenes) - a minor inconsistency, possibly an early draft title.

concerts.title: Grosses Unterhaltungskonzert (Grand Entertainment Concert)
concerts.venue: Camp concert hall (venue name not specified)

acts.act_name: Teil 1 (Part 1) - At the Piano: H. W. Katz

songs.title: Ouverture zu 'The Naughty Nineties'
songs.additional_notes: Overture.

songs.title: Walzer aus 'Der Graf von Luxemburg' (The Count of Luxembourg)

acts.act_name: Teil 2 (Part 2) - At the Piano: S. Cohn
Teil 3 (Part 3) - At the Piano: H. W. Katz

songs.title: Aus der Revue 'Wir reisen um die Welt'
songs.additional_notes: (a) Tango 'Ich hob' a Gspusi' (b) English Waltz 'Pour t'embrasser je donnerai ma vie'. Excerpted from the revue 'Wir Reisen um die Welt' (see that entry).

songs.title: Hawaiian Song
songs.lyrics: "The sea is so blue and the stars are so bright"

songs.title: Potpourri 'Von Heidelberg bis Richborough' (From Heidelberg to Richborough)
songs.additional_notes: Title references Heidelberg (a Melbourne suburb where Pietruschka later settled) and Richborough (a British refugee transit camp in Kent, near the Kitchener Camp, Sandwich) - a musical journey retracing the refugees' own displacement route.

concerts.additional_notes: One of several fully-documented Tatura camp concerts under M. Pietruschka's direction.

concerts.title: M. Pietruschka Chamber Concert (untitled programme)
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia

songs.title: Hungarian Dance No. 5
songs.additional_notes: A handwritten violin-part manuscript of a 'Hungarian Dance' also survives separately in the collection, likely Pietruschka's own performance part for this piece.

songs.title: 2 Ballads
songs.additional_notes: (a) 'Prince Eugen' by Carl Loewe; (b) 'The Campbells Are Comin'' (Old Scotch Ballad, traditional)

songs.title: Moto Perpetuo (Allegro di Concerto for 3 violins)

concerts.additional_notes: Printed note: 'Mr. Huttenbach kindly has lent his guitarre, built by himself at Hay' - confirming this concert took place in or shortly after internment at Hay Camp, where internees built their own instruments. This programme survives in duplicate in the collection (two separately scanned copies of the same document).

concerts.title: 1st Concert (Recreation Department)

acts.act_name: Selection of Light Classical Tunes (arr. M. Petroushka)

performers_list.Mr. Petroushka and his Orchestra: S. Cohn, A. Silberstein, J. Löwenhart, E. Hirsch

concerts.additional_notes: Cover features a watercolor of three wildflowers. Recreation Department heading matches other camp entertainment programmes in the collection ('Unterhaltungskonzert', 'Tripe & Ol'-Ozs', 'Hay-Fever'). This is among the earliest-dated items in the collection, confirming M. Pietruschka led an orchestra from very early in his internment.

concerts.title: Sergeant Snow White
alternate_titles: A Happy-Go-Lucky Musical Revue of Bad Old and Good New Times
concerts.venue: Union Theatre, University of Melbourne (University Grounds)
concerts.date: Two documented seasons: (1) April 15, 17, and 19, 1943 (the 19th being billed as the unit's official Anniversary Performance, by invitation); (2) May 11, 12, and 13, 1943 (repeat season).
concerts.presented_by: The 8th Australian Employment Company, A.M.F. (Australian Military Forces) - a labor unit largely composed of former 'enemy alien' internees (many Jewish refugees from Germany and Austria) based at Camp Pell, Melbourne, under Commanding Officer Capt. E. R. Broughton

overall_credits.band: The Band of the 8th Australian Employment Company, A.M.F., conducted by L'Cpl. O. Fleischer
overall_credits.printer: Riall Bros. Pty. Ltd., 282 Bay Street, Port Melbourne

songs.title: Overture
songs.based_on_tune: Some Day My Prince Will Come (from the Disney film 'Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs')
songs.additional_notes: Sung by a choir behind the curtain; curtain opens fully partway through; stage direction notes 'Grandma follows music - beating the rhythm.'

songs.title: Prologue (Grandma-Child dialogue)
songs.credits: Grandma - Pte. J. Feder; Child - Pte. L. Oppenheimer
songs.additional_notes: A frame-story device repeated at the start of each Act: Grandma narrates the fairy tale as an allegory of Nazi persecution, telling the Child of a time 'when nobody trusted nobody... even the good fairies were under suspicion... in danger of being destroyed forever,' and that Snowwhite was 'persecuted for her beauty - a refugee from the Queen's oppression.'

songs.title: The Mirror (scene)
songs.credits: Queen - O. H. Mayer; Master of the Bedchamber - Sgt. S. Lohde
songs.additional_notes: Extended comic dialogue built around a mock wartime radio news bulletin ('the A.P.C. Home Service'), satirizing propaganda broadcasts with jokes about Hitler awarding Mussolini 'the highest German order: The Double Cross,' Italian troops 'still leading by two lengths of spaghetti,' and other topical 1943 references, before the Queen finally asks the Mirror who is the fairest, and a Voice replies 'Never heard of Snow White???!!!???' The Queen is here called 'Queen Columina the Fifth', with a baby son 'Prince Katzi Watzi' on active service overseas.

songs.title: Fairy Wood (scene)
songs.credits: Snow White - Snow White; Prince Charming - Pte. Eric Liffmann; Longnose/Wolf - Pte. H. Reiss; The Witch - Cpl. A. P. Schmitz
songs.based_on_tune: 'One Song' (from the Disney film 'Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs') - Prince Charming's entrance song
songs.additional_notes: Comic scene in which the Witch tricks Longnose (searching for Snow White) into a kettle, from which a Wolf emerges - an explicit stage direction reveals 'Hitler appears' during a later reprise of this device (see Entr'acte, Act 2).

songs.title: The Banquet (scene)
songs.credits: Queen - O. H. Mayer; Master of the Bedchamber - Sgt. S. Lohde; 2 Ambassadors - Pte. S. Hochberger, E. Hutterer; Wolf - Pte. H. Reiss ('Wolf von Braunau' - a joke referencing Braunau am Inn, Hitler's birthplace); The Witch ('the strictly dishonourable Lady Wicked Witch') - Cpl. A. P. Schmitz; Prince Charming - Pte. Eric Liffmann; Master of the Tappers/Captain of the Guard - Pte. C. Begach; The Seven Dwarfs - The Swing Choir; The Ballet
additional_notes: Extended comic banquet scene: guests introduced with mock-diplomatic names, including French Ambassador 'Monsieur Antoine D'Omlette' (speaking in French wine/food puns) and Russian Ambassador 'Bolschillieff Wodkajeff Fedpoff Letmeoff.' Musical numbers within the scene: the 8th Swing Choir performs two songs, 'Sixty Seconds Got Together' and 'Mia Bella'; a tap dance by the 'Master of the Tappers'; Prince Charming sings 'Oh Maiden, My Maiden' by Franz Lehár (with a running joke that he is 'under contract' to Gaumont British, not to Snow White); a ball dance sequence (Gavotte-Conga-Gavotte); the Wolf sings an original song 'Won't You Come and Join My Axis', trying to recruit the Queen; Toy Soldiers dance; and the guests are arrested to the incidental strains of Chopin's Funeral March.

songs.title: Won't You Come and Join My Axis
songs.additional_notes: Original comic song in which the Wolf tries to persuade the Queen to join his 'axis'; she refuses, prompting his troops to arrest the assembled guests.

songs.title: Prologue (Grandma-Child, continued)
songs.credits: Grandma - Pte. J. Feder; Child - Pte. Oppenheimer
songs.additional_notes: Grandma narrates that the good fairies, Snowwhite, and the dwarfs were imprisoned by the witch and wolf, who 'started a great war'; a striking passage describes 'night had fallen over Europe and the voices of all the good fairies were silenced in the dungeons of tyranny. The tears that were shed in those days could have filled the seven seas - but in the memory of untold millions - the ideals of freedom and beauty lived on!!'

songs.title: Europe on the Air
songs.additional_notes: Sound-effects sequence: bassoon and kettledrum morse-code-like patterns represent radio stations calling out (London, Paris, Warsaw, Moscow, Praha, England, Russia, Austria, France, Czechoslovakia), building to a crescendo of 'EUROPE CALLING.' Background music transitions through 'It's a Long Way to Tipperary' (fading on the second verse) into the next scene.

songs.title: England: R. C. Sherriff's 'Journey's End'
songs.credits: Capt. Stanhope - Sgt. S. Lohde; Lieut. Hibbert - Pte. F. Gruen
songs.additional_notes: An 'Empire Broadcast Exchange' announcer introduces this as a broadcast of R. C. Sherriff's famous 1928 WWI play 'Journey's End,' quoting Sir Edward Grey's 1914 remark, 'The lights are going out all over Europe.' What follows is an extended, closely-adapted excerpt of the actual play (Act 2, Scene 3): Hibbert feigns illness to avoid returning to the front line, and Stanhope, revealing his own hidden fear, threatens him with his revolver before the two reconcile and resolve to face the coming attack together. [This scene is drawn substantially verbatim from Sherriff's copyrighted play text and is not reproduced in full here.]

songs.title: France: Bal Tabarin
songs.credits: The Chansonier - Pte. H. Blau; The Musician - Sgt. W. Baer; Les jeunes filles du Bal Tabarin / Les Spectateurs - The Ballet
songs.additional_notes: A 'Radio Paris' announcer sketch tours the 'Bal Tabarin' nightclub with dancers Nanon, Lola, Fifi, and Chichi. Pte. H. Blau sings two songs: (1) 'Je cherche après Titine'; (2) 'Paris, je t'aime'. Incidental music: the Marseillaise. Ballet: 'Thriller Girls'/'Fatties.'

songs.title: Russia: Melodies of Russia
songs.credits: Ivan - Pte. Eric Liffmann; 6 Russians - Sgt. W. Baer and 5 Accordionists
songs.additional_notes: Brief instrumental interlude featuring 8 accordions and a solo by Liffmann.

songs.title: Austria: Prater Dream
songs.credits: Alois Lerchengruber - Pte. H. Blau; 4 Admirers - The Ballet
songs.additional_notes: Set in Vienna's Prater; a Schrammel Quartet plays behind the scene. Blau sings two Viennese Heurigen (wine-tavern) songs: (1) 'Fein, fein schmeckt uns der Wein' (with a comic exchange about whether Austrians speak English); (2) 'Ich möchte mal wieder in Grinzing sein.' Ballet to 'Blue Danube' (J. Strauss): a mimed vignette of four lovers mistaking a gas-lit figure for their sweetheart, a sandwich-man revealing a health-warning poster, and a street-cleaner sweeping away discarded flower bouquets.

songs.title: Fein, fein schmeckt uns der Wein
Ich möchte mal wieder in Grinzing sein
Czechoslovakia: J. Hašek's 'The Good Soldier Schweyck'
songs.credits: Imperial Medical Officer - Sgt. S. Lohde; 3 Czech Patients: Palivetz - Dr. Deutsch, Pokorny - Pte. H. Fleischer, Schweyk - Pte. L. Komerner; Her Imperial Highness - Medical Orderly
songs.additional_notes: A 'Prague Calling' announcer sketch (background music: Smetana) introduces a comic scene adapted from Jaroslav Hašek's satirical novel 'The Good Soldier Švejk,' mocking absurd military medical malingering exams and cure-all 'enema' treatments, ending with Schweyk's ironic patriotic speech about serving the Emperor via enemas, 'and victory will be ours!!' [Adapted/derivative of Hašek's novel, not verbatim transcribed here.] Compare the much fuller separate stage production of this same story, 'Die Geschichte vom braven Soldaten Schwejk' (Tatura, September 1941, see that entry).

songs.title: Entr'acte: The Witch at Work
songs.credits: The Witch - Cpl. A. P. Schmitz; Wolf - Pte. H. Reiss
songs.additional_notes: A silent-march sequence (kettle drums) in which the Witch conjures a huge barbed-wire 'spider's web'; an explicit stage direction states 'Hitler appears' as the Wolf's true identity is revealed, ending with the Wolf's catchphrase 'Heil! Myself!'

songs.title: Germany: The Gestapo Office
songs.credits: Nazi Party Secretary (Kulak) - Sgt. S. Lohde; Solicitor ('Dr. Blizzard von Oz') - Pte. K. Loewenstein
songs.additional_notes: Signal music: 'Üb' immer Treu und Redlichkeit' (German patriotic hymn). A dark satirical scene: the solicitor pleads for Snowwhite's release from arrest, exposing the absurdity of Nazi racial bureaucracy (Aryan/'blood and snow' tests, objections that she lives unmarried with seven men). The scene ends when the Hitler portrait on the office wall comes to life and shoots both bureaucrats, each dying gasping 'Heil Hitler... to die... to die...' as the anthem signal plays slower and slower, ending on the word 'Redlichkeit' (integrity).

songs.title: The Cosmopolitan
songs.credits: Pte. L. Oppenheimer

songs.title: Poland: Chopin's Nocturne
songs.credits: 2 Polish Barons - Ptes. J. Chlunecky, F. Feuerstein; 2 Nazi Soldiers - L/Cpl. H. Davidson, Pte. F. Gruen
songs.additional_notes: London Signal introduces a Prime Minister's speech ('Germany has invaded Poland... we are at war with Germany'). Scene: an old castle garden with a 'Paderewski type' pianist (a nod to Ignacy Jan Paderewski) playing Chopin's Nocturne with a violinist as German soldiers invade; the soldiers shoot the musicians for 'signaling,' then in superstitious fright destroy the piano when the music mysteriously continues, believing it a ghost.

songs.title: The European
songs.credits: Sgt. S. Lohde

songs.title: The Ghosts of Europe
songs.credits: The Ballet-Ensemble; Soloist Cpl. A. P. Schmitz
songs.based_on_tune: 'Es ist ein langer Weg nach Budweis' (a parody of 'It's a Long Way to Tipperary,' substituting the Czech town Budweis)
songs.additional_notes: Ballet finale of Act 2: masked figures representing 'the tired, worn-out, vexed souls of Europe' rise from a burning/devastated scene (masks made by Pte. E. Fabian). This ballet also incorporated a recitation of the original poem 'Listen Brother, Listen Sister' by Pte. S. Hochberger (see below).

songs.title: Listen Brother, Listen Sister
songs.lyrics:
Listen Brother - listen sister;
From far comes the sound of their trampling feet,
Listen lady - listen mister;
They trample through courtyard and street.
They crumble your last bit of breath
They're crushing your world into graveyards of stone
They trample your freedom to death.
They're trampling, the earth is becoming sore,
Listen worker - listen soldier:
Their heels beat the rythm of war.

Listen brother - listen sister:
From far comes the sound of a clanking chain
Listen lady - listen mister:
Defenceless are tortured and slain.
The gallows of Europe reach out for the sky
The hangmen of Europe don't fall.
They're tapping the globe and they smile and stand by-
There are human bodies for sale.
Listen brother - listen sister:
The victims prepare a tremendous bill.
Listen worker - listen soldier:
The sound of the chain's getting shrill.

Listen brother - listen sister:
From far comes the sound of a thundering flame
Listen lady - listen mister:
To blast all you love is their game.
They storm through the land and they rave and they mob
Decaying is all in their grip
With poison and gun they are doing the job
Their tokens are death-head and whip
Listen brother - listen sister:
The fiend of all mankind has broken loose,
Listen worker - listen soldier:
His victims stand up and accuse.

Listen brother - listen sister:
From far comes the rhythmic sound of a drum
Listen lady - listen mister
The sound enters palace and slum
The conquered are stirring, they pull at their chain
A glint of new hope in their eyes -
They listen and find all their courage again
The peoples of Europe will rise....
Listen brother - listen sister:
The armies of freedom are on their way
Forward worker - forward soldier!
The hangman of Europe will P a y !!!!!
songs.additional_notes: An original anti-fascist protest poem, performed as part of 'The Ghosts of Europe' ballet finale; the sole credited author of an individual work in the entire production besides the composers.

songs.title: Prologue (Grandma-Child, concluded)
songs.additional_notes: Grandma tells how Snowwhite, the Seven Dwarfs, Prince Charming and the Queen all volunteered for the 'Home Forces' after the war spread 'all over the world' (with a topical mix-up joke, 'Atlantic Barter'/'Atlantic Charter'); the Child calls it the 'Eighth Enjoyment Company,' corrected by Grandma to 'Employment Company' - a direct, explicit reference to the real 8th Australian Employment Company presenting the show.

songs.title: The Dwarfs at the Wharves
songs.credits: Sergeant Staff - Sgt. S. Lohde; Private Charming - Pte. Eric Liffmann; The O.C. - O. H. Mayer; The Seven Private Dwarfs
songs.additional_notes: The Seven Dwarfs work as a wharf labor gang, pretending to work then playing 'two-up' (Australian gambling game) whenever supervision lapses - a pantomime titled 'The Two-Up Pennies' set to the tune 'Glow-Worm' ('Glühwürmchen') by Paul Lincke - and also break into poker games. A comic roll-call scene follows, with the Sergeant Staff unable to get the Dwarfs to count off correctly, and a bilingual German/English mess-kit-item joke. A romance subplot has the Queen (played here as a lovestruck servicewoman) flirt with Prince Charming to the entrance music 'Here Comes the Bride' (Wagner's Bridal Chorus), singing the duet 'You Are My Heart's Delight' by Franz Lehár before the pair exit for a tryst 'behind the oil drums.' The scene ends with the original song 'Pay Days.'

songs.title: Pay Days
songs.type: song within The Dwarfs at the Wharves
songs.additional_notes: An original song about payday in camp; text of the lyrics beyond the title/refrain cue was not fully captured in the surviving script.

songs.title: You Are My Heart's Delight
songs.type: song within The Dwarfs at the Wharves

songs.title: Music on Parade
songs.credits: The Band of the Eighth Employment Company

songs.title: The Sergeant Major
songs.credits: W. O. H. J. R. Stone; The Seven Private Dwarfs
songs.additional_notes: An extended comic drill scene: the Sergeant Major tries to drill the Seven Dwarfs, who repeatedly miscount and misdirect in unison sing-song responses ('Love-ly-Snow-white-Ser-geant-here!!!'); comic references to the real camp-transfer locations Tocumwal and Albury ('don't send us to Tocumwal, nor is Albury a place for us poor chaps of the Eighths!!!'); a Jewish-identity joke in which the Sergeant Major asks 'Anyone amongst you kosher?' and grants them leave for 'divine service' but warns them not to join the Christians for Church Parade on Sunday.

songs.title: On Arguing the Point
songs.credits: Pte. L. Oppenheimer (Philosophy Student monologue)
songs.additional_notes: The text of this monologue was not included in the surviving typescript (likely improvised or separately scripted).

songs.title: The Dwarfs at Home / The Tent
songs.credits: Private Charming - Pte. Eric Liffmann; The Seven Private Dwarfs; The Swing Choir
songs.additional_notes: An 'Evening in Camp' scene (fire picquets, tents rolled up, candlelight and stars) featuring two original songs. Followed by dialogue in which Prince Charming (offstage) calls for 'Sergeant Snowwhite,' wanting to take her to 'the Blue Triangle' (a real WWII servicemen's club/canteen), declaring 'I love her! I feel so unhappy....'

songs.title: We Are Sending a Letter to Our O.C.
songs.lyrics:
We are sending a letter to our O.C.,
A letter we hope he'll receive
And we wonder if he will please remember us
And will grant us Sydney leave.....
He gets a lot of letters for playthings,
From all the NCO's and boys,
But we want our leave - and quickly
Its better than all the toys.
And so we are sending an application
to our O.C.,
To grant Sydney home leave to us....
songs.additional_notes: A comic petition song about the men's desire for leave in Sydney.

songs.title: Eight's for 'Appy
songs.type: song (Solo Eric Liffmann & Choir) within The Tent
songs.lyrics:
When you are feeling low down
And when you've had a blow down
Take it easy as it comes and goes
When you're feeling chilly
And everything looks silly
Show that you can be right on your toes
Say Eight's for 'appy
When you feel snappy
And you don't want to cry
Say Eight's for 'appy
When you feel snappy
And you don't want to die
So you'd even give your ration card
For just a sight of Sergeant Snowwhite
Don't take it to your heart
Blow all your troubles away like bubbles
And start a little game without your girlie
You get up early
Oh what an awful shame
You're saying R for Rosenthal
B for Blumenduft and S for snappy
Now say EIGHT'S for 'APPY!!!!
songs.additional_notes: A direct rewrite of the earlier Hay Camp song 'Say Hay for Happy' (see 'Snowhite Joins Up' entry), substituting 'Eight's' (for the 8th Employment Company) for 'Hay,' updated to reflect the men's changed circumstances. Retains the original comic gag naming real fellow performers Rosenthal and Blumenduft.

songs.title: Orderly Room
songs.credits: The C.O. - O. H. Mayer; Sergeant Staff - Sgt. S. Lohde; 2 Soldiers ('Private X and Private Y') - Pte. F. Weidenbaum, F. Gruen; Committee Lady - E. Hutterer; The Toys - The Ballet
songs.based_on_tune: 'Ja, ja, der Leibessport' (German song about physical sport) - used as the Queen's dream music
songs.additional_notes: The Queen dozes at her desk and dreams of P.T. commands; two soldiers are court-martialed for disturbing her sleep with exercise commands but are acquitted after singing 'Physical Exercise Will Never Be Unwise' in their defense. A subsequent comic scene has 'Miss Doolittle,' secretary of 'The Society of Girlfriends'/Army Amenity Section, visit to arrange entertainment for the unit's first anniversary, with extended jokes calling the soldiers 'Jew-ropeans' (many 'from Austria,' nicknamed 'Austri-Aliens') who are nonetheless 'second to none' as soldiers. The Queen and Committee Lady then dance to 'Dance of the Toys' as toy gifts (some using period-typical, now offensive, naming conventions common to 1940s toy culture) are presented for the unit's anniversary.

songs.title: Physical Exercise Will Never Be Unwise
songs.lyrics:
Physical Exercise
Will never be unwise
It makes a thorough purge
Of every evil urge
Strong drink and money cares
Or sexes lurid snares
For me they're no temptation
Well, the last is somewhat an exaggeration.
Though I have never kissed
There's nothing I've missed
Even when Spring is there
And I'm alone with her
Into our English Grammar book
to study pronounciation
Well, the last is somewhat an exaggeration.

The war is at an end
And with it hitler's band
Shouting of super race
killing for living space
Reducing all to slaves
And serving only knaves
Have been exterminated
This, we hope, will soon be not exaggerated!!
songs.additional_notes: Sung in their own defense by two soldiers on trial for disturbing the Queen's sleep; the Queen finds them 'Not Guilty.'

songs.title: Happy Birthday to You
songs.lyrics:
Happy Birthday to you,
Happy Birthday to you,
Happy Birthday, 8th Employment,
Happy Birthday to you.
songs.additional_notes: Shirley Reid enters with a birthday cake to celebrate the 8th Employment Company's first anniversary. In the surrounding finale scene, running jokes reference the 1943 film 'Casablanca' and a mock 'peace conference' document; a stray 'Showgirl' wanders in from the wrong theatre ('The Tivoli is in Bourke Street!!!'); Private Charming bids the Queen farewell as he is discharged 'for work of national importance' with Snow White.

songs.title: Calling All Cobbers
songs.lyrics:
We are calling all Cobbers
For a smile that's bright
We are calling all Cobbers
Just because tonight's the night!!
When grey clouds are showing on top
Go - keep your courage well up!!
We are calling all Cobbers
For a Nation wide smile!!!!

[2nd Verse] We greet you Australia
Land of Sun and Peace
We are fighting for freedom
On the 7 seas - sev'n seas
When grey clouds are showing on top
Let's get our kitbags lined up
We are fighting for freedom
With a "V" for Victory smile!!!!!!!
songs.additional_notes: Closing ensemble number (Australian slang 'cobbers' = mates/friends). The second verse is a direct reworking of the 1941/42 Hay Camp song 'Good-bye to You, Australia' (see 'Snowhite Joins Up' entry), with the sentiment reversed from farewell to greeting/patriotic resolve. Followed by Grandma's closing narration: 'They got the big bad wolf in the end and the witch was caught too and, under strong guard, was sent to Dis-Appearia.... and so lived Snowwhite as a Sergeant in the Army amongst the freedom loving people until the day came - the day of victory!!!!' The show concludes with 'The King' (God Save the King).

concerts.additional_notes: This is the richest single item in the source collection: a complete 55-page typescript survives (supplemented by two shorter printed programmes for the April and May seasons respectively, and a surviving numbered invitation card for the 19 April 1943 Anniversary Performance addressed from 'Capt. E. R. Broughton, C.O., and the Officers of the 8th Australian Employment Company,' RSVP to 'Warrant Officer H. J. R. Stone, Chairman of the Committee, 8th Aust. Employment Coy., Camp Pell, Melbourne'). It is a direct expansion of the earlier revue 'Snowhite Joins Up' (Hay Internment Camp, New Year 1941/42, see that entry), created by the same writer-director (Doc K. Sternberg) and composer (Ray Martin), reusing the Snow White fairy-tale frame and several songs with updated lyrics reflecting the men's changed circumstances (from internees anticipating deportation to enlisted soldiers of the 8th Employment Company). The production blends pantomime, wartime allegory, a mock radio news bulletin, an extended excerpt of R. C. Sherriff's 'Journey's End,' a scene from Hašek's 'The Good Soldier Švejk,' the original poem 'Listen Brother, Listen Sister,' and multiple original camp-written songs, interwoven with a wide range of borrowed popular and classical tunes (Disney, Lehár, J. Strauss, Chopin, Wagner, Paul Lincke, 'Tipperary,' French chansons, and Viennese Heurigen songs).

concerts.title: Journey's End
concerts.date: Not specified (the play's internal setting is March 1918, WWI - not the performance date)

overall_credits.author: R. C. Sherriff
overall_credits.produced_by: M. Bittermann (partially obscured by a later handwritten dedication overlay)

acts.act_name: Act III (10-minute interval after Act II)

concerts.additional_notes: A full production of R. C. Sherriff's 1928 WWI drama, distinct from the brief excerpt of the same play performed within 'Sergeant Snow White' (1943), which used Sgt. S. Lohde as Stanhope and Pte. F. Gruen as Hibbert. Presumably an earlier, separate full staging by the same broader internee theatrical community. A later handwritten dedication was added to the programme, partly legible: '...Dank fuer die schoenen Weihnachtsabende Dein Micha...' ('thanks for the lovely Christmas evenings, yours Micha...') and, at the bottom, 'All the best, yours...' - suggesting this programme was kept as a personal keepsake.

concerts.title: Free Concerts for the Forces - Jeanne Gautier & Raymond Lambert
concerts.presented_by: Australian Broadcasting Commission, series 'Free Concerts for the Forces' (open to members of the fighting services) - Seventh concert of the 1944 series

songs.title: Ballad in D Minor, Op. 10, No. 1
Sonata in D Minor (Allegro; Adagio; Con Sentimento; Presto Agitato)
Sonata in C Minor, Op. 45 (Allegro; Romanza; Allegro)
Chants d'Espagne (Montagnesa; Tonada Murciana; Saeta [Invocation]; Granadina)

concerts.additional_notes: The programme sheet also advertises the following week's concert (24 March 1944, 8:15pm): the A.B.C. Melbourne Orchestra conducted by Lionel Lawson with Bertha Jorgensen (violinist) - not part of this concert.

concerts.title: Free Concerts for the Fighting Services - Edward Goll & Thea Philips
concerts.presented_by: Australian Broadcasting Commission, series 'Free Concerts for the Fighting Services' - Eleventh concert of the series

songs.title: Sonate in E Flat (Allegro; Adagio; Finale-Presto)

concerts.title: Klavier Abend (Piano Evening) - Peter Stadlen

songs.title: Sonate As-dur, Op. 26 (Andante von Variazioni; Scherzo [Allegro molto]; 'Sulla morte d'un Eroe'; Allegro)
Trois mouvements de Petrouchka (a. Danse Russe; b. Chez Petrouchka; c. La semaine grasse)

concerts.additional_notes: Peter Stadlen (1910-1996) was an Austrian-British pianist and later music critic (Daily Telegraph), interned in Australia as an 'enemy alien' during WWII (at Hay and Tatura camps) before returning to the UK - directly paralleling M. Pietruschka's own history. This programme survives in triplicate in the source collection.

concerts.title: Piano Recital - Peter Stadlen (Three Sonatas)
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia

songs.title: Sonata in B Flat Major (Allegro moderato; Andante sostenuto; Allegro vivace con delicatezza; Allegro)
Sonata in E Major, Op. 109 (Vivace ma non troppo; Prestissimo; Andante con variazioni)
Sonata in C Major (Allegro moderato; Andante cantabile; Allegretto)

concerts.additional_notes: See also the separate 'Klavier Abend' Peter Stadlen programme (different repertoire) in this collection.

concerts.title: Hay-Fever (Camp Theatre)
concerts.type: play (theatrical production, likely Noel Coward's 'Hay Fever')
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia
concerts.additional_notes: Only the cover and back-cover pages survive; no cast list or scene/song details are present in the collection.

concerts.title: Unterhaltungskonzert (Entertainment Concert)
concerts.additional_notes: Only the decorative cover survives (Recreation Department heading, crown emblem, treble clef with comedy/tragedy masks). No interior programme details captured.

concerts.title: Tripe & Ol'-Ozs (A Variety Fare)
concerts.additional_notes: Only the decorative cover survives. No interior programme details captured.

concerts.title: Vitamin Variety Show (untitled)

overall_credits.produced_by: 'The Pure Products Pty. Ltd', Managing Director 'W.A.B.'

songs.title: Vitamin A: 'Keep it Dark' / 'Uncle Tom on Tap'
Vitamin B: 'Another Disillusion' / 'With the Conjuror's Compliments'
Vitamin E: 'The Spice of Life' / 'Paprika on the Piano'
On the Playing Fields - an Opening Chorus

concerts.additional_notes: Only one interior page survives; the overall show title and full cast were not captured.

concerts.title: Hebraeische Lieder, gesungen beim Oneg Schabbat (Hebrew Songs Sung at the Oneg Shabbat)

acts.act_name: Traditional Sabbath songs (transliterated, with German translations)

songs.title: Shalom Aleichem
songs.additional_notes: German translation given: 'Gegruesst, ihr dienenden Engel...' ('Greetings, you serving angels...').

songs.title: Eliyahu Hanavi
songs.additional_notes: About the prophet Elijah; German translation given.

songs.title: Hine Ma Tov
songs.additional_notes: 'Behold how good and pleasant it is for brothers to dwell together'; German translation given.

songs.title: Halleluja
songs.additional_notes: German translation given.

songs.title: Additional Sabbath table-song verses (untitled fragments)
songs.additional_notes: Continuing Kiddush-related verses on the booklet's final pages.

concerts.additional_notes: A small songbook booklet (pages numbered 2-8) with a decorative Star of David and Sabbath candles/kiddush cup illustration on the cover, documenting a communal religious/cultural song evening distinct from the collection's many secular theatrical productions.

concerts.title: Kitchener Camp Orchestral Concert
concerts.additional_notes: Kitchener Camp (Sandwich, Kent) was a real transit camp that sheltered roughly 4,000 Jewish male refugees fleeing Nazi Germany and Austria in 1939, shortly before WWII. Only the announcement page survives (no detailed repertoire). Printed by T. F. Pain & Sons, Sandwich & Deal. 'All Welcome.' Places the collection's refugee-musician community in England prior to the 1940 deportation to Australia aboard the Dunera.

concerts.title: We're In Love!!! (A Rumbling Riot of Rollicking Revue)
concerts.venue: The First Class Lounge, H.M.T. D3

overall_credits.music_written_and_arranged_by: Ray Martin, assisted by Eddie Kassner
overall_credits.featuring: The Stirling Singers, conducted by Kurt Behrens

songs.title: The Man in the Cupboard
songs.additional_notes: 'Is the coast clear'

songs.title: Maori Haka
songs.additional_notes: 'New Zealand on parade'

songs.title: The Price of Sin
songs.additional_notes: One Act Opera by 'all sorts of people'

songs.title: The Silly Syncopators
songs.additional_notes: 'Better than ever'

songs.title: On the Dole
songs.additional_notes: 'Easy Money'

songs.title: The Six Cupids
songs.additional_notes: 'Aiming at your heart'

songs.title: Max and Meechie
songs.additional_notes: Description blacked out/censored in the original document.

songs.title: The Saddest Man in the World
The Siren
songs.additional_notes: 'There she goes again'

songs.title: The Fixer
songs.composer_lyricist: Michael Mellinger, Ray Martin, and Alan Ford

concerts.additional_notes: Cover carries a handwritten note giving the ship's route and date: 'Australia, Auckland (N-Z), Panama Canal, Liverpool - End 1941.' Ray Martin here is very likely the same composer credited for 'Special Musical Arrangements' in 'Sergeant Snow White' (1943) and music in the companion shipboard concert 'V for Victory - Variety' (10 days earlier on the same voyage, see that entry); Max Lewinsky also recurs across both shows and the earlier 'Snowhite Joins Up.' Survives in duplicate in the collection.

concerts.title: V for Victory - Variety (Ship's Concert)
concerts.type: shipboard variety concert with one-act play
concerts.venue: The First Class Lounge, H.M.T. D3

acts.act_name: The Man in the Bowler Hat (A Comedy Thriller by A. A. Milne)

songs.title: Full play
songs.credits: Produced by Peter Land; Stage Manager B. Bell. Cast: Mary - Mrs. O. Keel; John - George Prager; '???' - Nick Beard; Hero - Peter Land; Heroine - Margaret Keel; The Chief Villain - Michael Mellinger; The Bad Man - Ernest Gumperz.

songs.title: A Piano, a Boy and a Song

concerts.additional_notes: Occurred 10 days before 'We're In Love!!!' (11 November 1941, see that entry) on the same voyage, sharing much of the same shipboard entertainment community (Peter Land, Ray Martin, Max Lewinsky, Ted Fell, Michael Mellinger, Ernest Gumperz, Capt. O'Gorman).

concerts.title: Stars of the Seven Seas (A Non Stop Variety Show)
concerts.location: Aboard T.S.S. "Largs Bay" (Captain T. V. Roberts, R.D., R.N.R.)
concerts.date: Undated; a pencil drawing on the reverse of the programme is captioned 'Going Home! 16.7.41' (16 July 1941), possibly indicating the voyage date
concerts.presented_by: In aid of the British Red Cross Society
concerts.additional_notes: Only the cover survives; no cast/song list. The reverse bears a pencil drawing of a man riding a sea-serpent, captioned 'Going Home! 16.7.41.'

concerts.title: 8th Aust. Employment Coy March
concerts.date: Not specified (contemporaneous with the unit's 1943 activities)
concerts.lyrics:
We know the world from Norway down to Greece
We left our homes and sailed the Seven Seas;
We answered the call, when they asked for volunteers
For the Home Defence as Pioneers
In Austra-li-a-a-a.

When a ship comes in then we take the freight ashore,
And we work like hell as we've never done before.
We shift bales and cases and we handle them with skill,
And enjoy the daily drill
In the e-ve-ning, ching, ching,
In the e-ve-ning.

We are fond of marching and we always "shake it up",
Trotting like the horses at a rainy Melbourne Cup.
Smartly we jump out when the morning bugle goes
And there roar those N.C.O's
Those N-C-O-oh-ohs
Those N-C-Os!

Give us our Leave Pass and we give you a fair go
Give us a long spell and let us have a blow;
Then you'll be surprised how efficient we can be
The Employment Company
The Eighth Employ-oy-oy-
-ment Company.

We have lots of fun, when we flirt and when we dance;
We don't like C.B. [Confined to Barracks], when we go in for romance,
But Absent Without Leave does not enter our minds,
When we serve in our lines
With stew and bullybeef
With bullybeef.

Ev'rybody thinks we're a funny looking crowd,
Being in the Army makes us feeling mighty proud,
We adore the Skipper and salute the Rising Sun,
Until Victory is won.
For Peace and Victory,
FOR VICTORY!
concerts.additional_notes: The official/unofficial marching anthem of the 8th Australian Employment Company, written by the same Sgt. Lohde who plays the recurring Sergeant/Queen's-attendant roles throughout 'Sergeant Snow White.' The opening line's reference to sailing 'from Norway down to Greece' over 'the Seven Seas' reflects the international refugee backgrounds of the unit's men.

concerts.title: The Ceramic Reparteg Club Presents: A Fruity Melodramma
concerts.date: 6 November 1941 (handwritten date on programme; signature partly reads 'J.S.')

overall_credits.music_and_sound_effects: 'Professor Guiseppe Leggitonio' (comic pseudonym, 'Famous Impresario and Conductor of the Footscray Philharmonic Orchestra')
overall_credits.production_seduction_abduction_by: 'The Venerable Linseed Saliva' (comic pseudonym, 'Amazing Necromancer - Prestidigitateur of Nuisance fame')

acts.act_name: Act I (Scenes I-V, all set at 'Hardrow Hall')

songs.title: Scene I
songs.additional_notes: Hardrow Hall

songs.title: Scene II
songs.additional_notes: ditto

songs.title: Scene III
songs.additional_notes: ditto

songs.title: Scene IV
songs.additional_notes: ditto

songs.title: Scene V
songs.additional_notes: ditto ('Say!! can't we get out of this bleedin' place.')

cast.Murgatroyd Murdock, alias the gum-shoe m: played by 'Baron Heindrich Isaacstein von Reichardscraad (Hitler's last hope)'

concerts.additional_notes: A satirical, comically over-the-top amateur melodrama with joke pseudonyms substituted for real performers' names throughout, including a topical Hitler joke in the leading man's fictional 'real' name. Notice: 'For the comfort of patrons, no ball playing will be allowed during the performance.' Text is cut off at the bottom of the surviving page.

concerts.title: Sandstorms / Hay Days (song manuscript)
concerts.location: Hay Internment Camp, New South Wales, Australia

songs.title: Sandstorms
songs.lyrics:
Boo-ooh - There it goes once more
Rattling at your door -
Come watch out
There's no doubt,
Listen to the roar:
Sandstorms, sandstorms,
Nothing else but sandstorms
Blowing sand right into my heart, -
Sandstorms, sandstorms,
You don't know how sandstorms,
When it breaks your heart apart.
Cry, about the old affair,
Cry, but what's the use to care,
Sandstorms, sandstorms
Bringing back those grand storms,
When I loved with you, sweetheart.
songs.additional_notes: Likely evokes the notorious dust storms of Hay, NSW - a nostalgic love song using the storms as metaphor.

songs.title: Hay Days
songs.lyrics:
Hay Days, Hay Days
Make your Hay Days your play days
Sing on every day, doodle-oodle-aye
Smile with never a care.
Hay Days, Hay Days
Make your Hay Days your gay days,
Yours is all the fun, yours is all the sun
For it shines everywhere.
As long as we're all pals and we're together
Never mind the rains and stormy weather.
I know, you know, troubles come but they do go,
Doors will open up, you'll be right on top;
Live your life your own way
Calling every day 'Hay Day'.
songs.additional_notes: This is the draft manuscript of the song performed (with identical lyrics) in the revue 'Snowhite Joins Up' (Hay Camp, New Year 1941/42) - see that entry.

concerts.additional_notes: A handwritten manuscript on ruled paper containing drafts of these two songs.
